coredata.dc:title: The role of electron–phonon interaction on the transport properties of graphene based nano-devices
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Mogulkoc, A.
Modarresi, M.
Kandemir, B.S.
Roknabadi, M.R.
Shahtahmasebi, N.
Behdani, M.
coredata.dc:description: Transport through a nano-device in the presence of electron–phonon interaction has been studied by using unitary-transformation scheme together with the non-equilibrium Green׳s function formalism. The non-interacting Hamiltonian is written within the tight binding model and left/right electrodes are described in wide-band approximation. The interaction between electrons is studied in the mean-field Hubbard model. Our results indicate that both phonon-restricted and phonon-assisted transport through two different configurations of graphene quantum dot occur. Also, the electron–phonon interaction screens the electron–electron interaction, and reduces the spin polarization.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Electronic transport
Holstein–Hubbard model
Electron–phonon interaction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Rectangular and (b) triangular graphene quantum dots between two semi-infinite electrodes in the presence of electron–phonon interaction.
Eigen-states of R-GQD in the presence of electron–phonon interaction in the Holstein model.
The transmission spectrum of R-GQD for different structures in the presence of electron–phonon interaction.
The current–voltage characteristic of different R-GQD in the presence of electron–phonon interaction.

Variation of spin up (blue) and down (red) eigen-states for T-GQD with electron–phonon coupling constant strength. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The I–V curve for T-GQD in the presence of electron–phonon interaction with different strengths.
The variation of spin polarization as a function of bias voltage in the presence of electron–phonon interaction through the T-GQD.

head.ce:title: The role of electron–phonon interaction on the transport properties of graphene based nano-devices

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Ankara University, Ankara, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad, Mashhad, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Transport through a nano-device in the presence of electron–phonon interaction has been studied by using unitary-transformation scheme together with the non-equilibrium Green׳s function formalism. The non-interacting Hamiltonian is written within the tight binding model and left/right electrodes are described in wide-band approximation. The interaction between electrons is studied in the mean-field Hubbard model. Our results indicate that both phonon-restricted and phonon-assisted transport through two different configurations of graphene quantum dot occur. Also, the electron–phonon interaction screens the electron–electron interaction, and reduces the spin polarization.
We study the effect of electron–phonon interaction on the transport properties of two different configurations of graphene quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:para: With synthesis of graphene [1], this interesting two-dimensional nanostructure has become one of the hot topics for condensed matter physicist. The electron–phonon interaction is important to understand the electrical and optical properties of nano-structures. There are many studies which are devoted the effects of electron–phonon interaction on properties of graphene [2–8]. Recently different graphene nano-structures such as carbon nano-tube, graphene nano-ribbon and graphene quantum dots attract considerable interest.

The transport through a nano-device is an interesting problem for scientist. The well-known non-equilibrium Green׳s function is a powerful model to study the transport through a nano-structure between two semi-infinite leads [9–15]. In this work, two different configurations of graphene quantum dot in the rectangular and triangular shapes between two semi-infinite leads have been considered. We focused on triangular and rectangular GQD, which yield energy band gap independently of the geometry. Not only geometry but also edge states specify the electronic properties of whole structure. For instance, triangular GQD have zero energy states which is related to symmetry breaking of A (B) sub-lattice [16,17]. The hexagonal and triangle shape [18] and other graphene quantum dots were synthesized in the laboratory [19–21]. Besides the effects of geometry and edge states, the electronic properties of the structure can be controlled with electron–phonon coupling, i.e., I–V curve, energy gap. The non-interacting Hamiltonian is written in the tight-binding approach and the effect of left/right leads is described by using the self-energy operator in the wide-band approximation. In this approximation the real part of self-energy is neglected and the imaginary part is set to a constant number [22–24]. We study the effect of electron–phonon interaction in the Holstein model on the transport properties of graphene quantum dots between two semi-infinite leads. In the case of strong coupling between dot and leads the electron–phonon interactions are almost negligible [25,26]. As a result, we use the intermediate coupling strength between molecule and electrodes. Previous works on graphene show that one can control the electron–phonon coupling constant by ad-atom [27] and external electric field [2]. In the triangular dot the breaking of sub-lattice symmetry due to the presence of zigzag edge produces a net finite macroscopic magnetization [24,28–30]. The Holstein–Hubbard model (HHM) considers on-site coupling of the electrons with a dispersion-less lattice vibration mode together with Hubbard interaction [31–34]. Even some studies consider the Holstein Model in graphene with out-of plane distortion [35–37], this study is the first approach which examines the Hubbard model and electron–phonon interaction in means of Holstein Model.

We consider two successive methods to investigate the whole coupled phonon–electron system of both rectangular and triangular shapes. First treatment is based on a unitary transformation method in graphene nano-structures to obtain an effective Hamiltonian for dot in the presence of interaction. [6–8] And the other one is the non-equilibrium Green׳s function method to calculate the transport properties of nano-device in the presence of electron–electron and electron–phonon interactions.

The total Hamiltonian of the system contains the non-interacting Hamiltonian, i.e., the electron–electron and the coupling between dot and the left/right leads.
(1)

is the hopping between nearest neighbor carbon atoms in the GQD.

C
i , σ
) is the creation (annihilation) operator for an electron with spin σ at site i of the GQD. The last term in Eq. (2) is the Hubbard model for describing the electron–electron interaction with strength of U. We only consider the interaction between electrons with opposite spin on the same site in the GQD. The Hubbard Hamiltonian can be discussed in the mean-field sense and configuration method. The mean field approach was previously applied for electron–electron interaction in the case of Hartree–Fock approximation [38–41]. In the mean-field model for the Hubbard Hamiltonian the electron–electron interaction term is approximated as U

Another term in Eq. (1) is the coupling between GQD and left/right (L/R) leads [14,15]

) is the creation (annihilation) operator for an electron with spin σ at site i of left/right leads and

is the coupling constant between quantum dot and leads. It was shown that the strength of coupling constant between molecule and leads plays an important role in the electronic and transport properties of nano-structures [43–45]. The effect of left/right electrodes are fully described by defining the self-energy operator of left/right leads

is the surface Green׳s function for left/right electrode. The self-energy in the matrix form has non-zero elements only for sites in the edge of quantum dot coupled to left and right electrodes [46]. Green׳s function of the GQD between the left/right electrodes is written as [1,9,10,12,14,15,23,24]

. By applying the bias voltage to the left/right electrodes, the difference between the left/right Fermi distributions produces a net current in the device.

In the presence of electron–phonon interaction the total Hamiltonian is expressed as

which is also known as the Hubbard–Holstein Hamiltonian. In Eq. (6), second and third terms correspond to unperturbed phonon field and electron–phonon interaction, respectively. Here, ℏ

is the electron number operator, and g is the coupling parameter of the interaction. We consider Lang–Firsov type unitary transformation [47]:

] is the effective hopping parameter [48] and is also responsible for the small polaron formation in the system [49]. Also, in the presence of electron–phonon interaction the coupling matrix between GQD and electrodes is changed. And the new self-energy of left/right electrodes is

[13,50] leads to a Landauer-like equation for current in the interacting system [13]

In the case of weak interaction, i.e. Λ → I and the Landauer–Büttiker regime is restored. The
G
σ
r
is the retarded Green׳s function of GQD between leads in the presence of electron–phonon interaction which differs from the non-interacting one. The electron–phonon interaction changes the Green׳s function, self-energy of electrodes and introduce the Λ in the current equation.

We study the rectangular and the triangle shape of GQDs between two semi-infinite electrodes as shown in Fig. 1. The on-site energy and hopping between nearest carbon atoms are set to
ε
c
= 0 and
t
c − c
= 2.7
eV , respectively. The Hubbard parameter is U = 1.1
t
c − c
and the imaginary part of self-energy is Im Σ = − 0.15
t
c − c
.

The R-GQD is characterized by determining the N
X by N
Y where N
X and N
Y show the length and width of the nano-structure, respectively (Fig. 1a). Fig. 2 shows the variation of eigen-states of R-GQD in the presence of electron–phonon interaction in the Holstein model. The electron–phonon interaction decrease the hopping parameter with an exponential factor, as a result the band width and electronic gap are decreased. Fig. 3 shows the counter-plot of transmission spectrum in the nano-device for different configurations of R-GQD. The value of electron–phonon coupling is changed for g=0 to g=0.1eV. The g=0 is equivalent to the non-interacting situation. By increasing the length and width of the rectangular, transmission through the structure decreases and increases, respectively. The presence of electron–phonon interaction decreases the energy width and the transmission through the R-GQD. Transmission spectrum has many peaks which are occurred in the eigenvalues of the dot. The height and broadening of each peak is determined by the amount of the imaginary part of the self-energy operator. Fig. 4 shows the current–voltage characteristic of different configurations in the presence of different values of electron–phonon coupling. By increasing the applied voltage new states contribute in the current and the transmission peaks lead to the step-like form in the I–V curve. The electron–phonon interaction decreases current in the GQD. Both transmission and current–voltage curve show the phonon-restricted transport through the R-GQD in the presence electron–phonon interaction. By increasing the strength of interaction the steps in the I–V curves in Fig. 4 are disappeared, and Ohm׳s law is restored in a nano-device. The electron–phonon interaction plays different roles in different configurations of the quantum dots which is related to the geometry of the nano-structure. The importance of the coupling constant between the molecule and electrodes on the transport properties of nano-devices is shown in previous works [25,26]. Here, the coupling strength is determined by using the self-energy operator. By increasing the imaginary part of the retarded self-energy of left/right electrodes the Λ → I and the effect of electron–phonon interaction is decreased. In the other word, the effect of electron–phonon interaction is significant in the case of weak coupling between molecule and electrodes and it is almost negligible in the case of strong coupling limit [25,26].

The structure of T-GQD is specified by just N as shown in Fig. 1b. Fig. 5 shows the variation of eigen-states of T-GQD versus electron–phonon coupling constant. The main difference between the R-GQD and T-GQD is related to the spin polarization of electronic structure of the triangle dot which leads to separation between up and down states. Due to the sub-lattice symmetry breaking in the triangular shape there is a net finite magnetic moment in the zigzag edge of the dot [24,28–30]. In this case the Hubbard interaction plays a crucial role in the electronic structure of the nano-device. Here, we use the mean-field approach to solve the Hubbard term in the total Hamiltonian. We assume that half of the electronic orbitals are filled with electrons which mean the system is half-filled. The presence of electron–electron interaction splits the degenerate states in the Fermi level for spin up and down, and it produces a net finite magnetization in the T-GQD.

Due to the spin dependent electronic structure of T-GQD, the current through dot is spin polarized. The total current is the sum over the spin up and down currents,
I
t o t
=
I
↑
+
I
↓
. Fig. 6 shows the effect of electron–phonon interaction on the total current in the different configurations of T-GQD. In this case the localized states around the Fermi level do not contribute in transport. By increasing the strength of interaction, the peaks in the I–V curve are disappeared in the T-GQD. In the low voltage region, current increases by increasing the electron–phonon coupling constant. Unlike to the R-GQD, the phonon-assisted transport is observed through the T-GQD in the low voltage region. The phonon-assisted transport was studied in other nano-structures [51–54]. However the electron–phonon interaction decreases the current in the higher voltages. There is a critical threshold for the applied voltage to switch to the phonon-restricted transport in the T-GQD which depends on the strength of electron–phonon coupling constant. A phonon-assisted process can be dominant for nano-structures through the polaronic transport [51]. Franck–Condon blockade regime which is effective at low bias voltage with strong electron–phonon coupling may be responsible for this phonon-assisted transport phenomenon [55,56]. It can be easily seen in Fig. 6, phonon-assisted regions are increased to the higher currents with increasing g factor at low bias voltages. We define the spin polarization ration as S P = [ (
I
↑
−
I
↓
) / (
I
↑
+
I
↓
) ] to show the spin dependent feature of the transport. According to definition, the maximum value of SP is 1 which is equivalent to the pure spin up current. The variation of SP with respect to the bias voltage for three different sizes of T-GQD is shown in Fig. 7. Our results show a relatively large SP in the T-GQD which is related to the magnetic moments in the edge of dot. The negative value of SP in certain voltage interval shows that the dominant current is carried out by electron with spin down through the dot. The SP is approximately unchanged in the low regime g, however it decreases dramatically in the case of large electron–phonon coupling constant and it reaches to a constant value in the higher voltages. Here, the electron–phonon interaction screens the interaction between electrons and decreases the SP.

In summary, we consider R-T GQDs between two semi-infinite leads in the presence of electron–phonon interaction. We consider the electron–electron and electron–phonon interactions in the Hubbard and Holstein models, respectively. By using the famous Lang–Firsov type unitary transformation, we reach to an effective Hamiltonian which is solved in the mean-field model. The current through the structure is calculated by using the well-known non-equilibrium Green׳s function formalism. Our results show the phonon-restricted and phonon-assisted transport which may be related to the Franck–Condon blockade through the R-GQD and T-GQD, respectively. However, the values of electron–phonon couplings are considered at the same order, the effects of electron–phonon coupling are more dominant on T-GQD with respect to R-GQD, yield phonon assisted transport phenomena. In addition, we show that the electron–phonon interaction suppresses the spin-polarization in the triangular shape.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: One of the authors (B.S. Kandemir) acknowledges support for this work under TUBİTAK Grant No. 113F103.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: On the Electron-Phonon Interactions in Graphene, in Low-Dimensional Functional Materials
Quantum Statistical Mechanics: Green׳s Function Methods in Equilibrium and Nonequilibrium Problems
Eur. Phys. J. B: Condens. Matter Complex Syst.
